u. S. PACIFIC FLEET HEAD
QUARTERS, Guam, March 2,—
(APv—Tank-led U. S. Marines
hammered out gains on smoking
two Jima, compressing the des
perate Japanese garrison into the
rugged northern tip for a final
stand. Although the situation is
well in hand it is a bloody battle
with a high death toll.
Maj. Gen. Erskine’s Third Div
ision occupies the western end of
Motoyama and have siezed both
air strips.
• Maj. Gen. Rockey’s tanks pushed
forward routing the few remain
ing Jap-held hill positions.
NEW FIRE
BOMBS ON
TOKYO
UNDATED, March 2,—(AP)—The
* Japanese radio TOKYO, today
warned the Japanese homeland
that the U. S. Superfortresses
were dropping phosphorous in
cendiaries with high destructive
power, and bombs which spread
fire considerably greater than
any previously dropped.
; AIRFIELDS
FOR 2nd
. DIVISION
JUNEAU, March 2,—(AP)—The
Senate introduced a joint mem
orial prepared by Senators Lyng
and Cochran, that the Highway
Engineer spend $10,000 in road
imporvements and airfield for
Candle, $10,000 for roads and air
• field at Fortuna Ledge, $2,500
for trails and airfields at each
at Shungnak, Kiana and Selawik.
SEABEES IN REVIE W—Vice Admiral W. L. Calhoun, CSN, commander service force. U. S.
Pacific Fleet (center) salutes the colors as he reviews Third Naval Construction Regiment at Hono.
lulu Naval Air station. At right of Adm. Calhoun is Commodore John R. Perry (CEC) CSN, olhcer
tn-charge of Hawaiian area construction brigades. At left, Lt. Com. R. P. Redman, battalion commander.
WHITE RUS
ARMY IN
STETTIN
LONDON, March 2,—(AP>—Mar
shal Rokossovsky’s Second White
Russian Army has Stettin on the
Dansig highway 7 miles from the
Baltic coast and have thus sealed
off Dansig from any escape.
This latest penetration also
broke into the main German lines
north of Arnswalde and crossed
the Isna river which forms the
chief defense for the fortress city
of Stargard.
Another offensive has been an
nounced 125 miles northeast of
Prague, headed toward the Su
deten mountains.
IGLOO No.l HAS
GRAND PARTY
The Pioneers of Alaska, Igloo
No. 1, held their regular meeting
at the Whaley House, with Pres
ident Ole Hay presiding. This
newly elected officer is always
cooking up something to surprise
9,h ARMY \
15 MILE
ADVANCE
PARIS, March 2, —(AP>—Swift
and powerful was the American
Ninth Army as it made a spec
tacular 15 mile advance, running
over into the Rhur valley taking
the rail center of Krefeld and the
great Rhine port of Duisburg.
The 83rd Infantry fought
through Neuss on the west bank
poposite Dusseldorf.
Other towns along the route
surrendered as shells poured into
Dusseldorf.
General Simpson’s shock forces
spurred ahead into Velno and are
within 16 miles of the Canadian
forces and are threatening to trap
thousands of Germans among
which are come of Germany’s fin
est parachute troops.
the members and this time it was
home made chicken tamales.
He prevailed upon Louie Polet
a newly initiated member, to fur
nish the tamales as he had heard
Louie really could make them.
Everyone enjoyed the surprise j
and licked the platter clean.
WALLACE
IS NEW SEC
COMMERCE
WASHINGTON, March 2,—(AP)
—Henry A. Wallace today became
official Secretary of Commerce.
He announced that his depart
ment would do its best to facil
itate the maximum cooperation
between nations.
He will receive $15,000. a year.
NO COOLIE
LABOR IN
ALASKA
WASHINGTON, March 2,—(AP)—
The people of Alaska deserve the
right to make a living by fishing
declared Representative Rankin,
when describing the $60,000,000
industry, and claimd he found no
evidence of ‘Coolie labor’ de
priving Alaskans of a living, in
his recent survey.
•>
SURPRISE
INVASION
MANILA, March2,—(AP)—Am
erican invasion forces landed
yesterday on Palawan in the
westernmost Philippines and
quickly captured the main port
and proceeded to completely oc
cupy the entire island.
This siezure will give the U.S.
Navy domination over the China
Sea, with approaches to Japan
held Indo-China, Thailand, Ma
laya and Singapore.
The attack was a complete sur
prise and now gives the Amer
icans control of the fifth lar
250 miles south of Manila.
General MacArthur said the
enemy was so engrossed else
where that he failed to diagnose
our plans and was not prepared
for the attack.
FDR SAYS
NO MIDDLE
COURSE
WASHINGTON, March 2,—(AP>
—President Roosevelt, in his
talk before the joint session told
Congress that it was in their
hands as responsibility for fut
ure peace or another world com
flict. He said, “There is no
middle course.
The Chief Executive said that
the meet at Yalta was a turning
point in American history. .
The President voiced again
his ultimatum of unconditional
surrender for Germany.
He also stated that the poten
tial source of trouble will be Po
land, and that no decision was
reached in Yalta on the subject.
_ •
CHILDREN GO TO
ORTHOPEDIC
Three children left via the
Pan American Airways plane for
entrance to the Orthopedic Hos
pital in Seattle. They were;
Teddy Reed Jr., William Lay
ton, and Robert Singyke.

'ed which excluded all Chinese

Right there smuggling them
in became a business, which has
increased from year to year until
today it is said that there is a
regularly organized ring whose
business is to get them into the
United States. They come in via
the box car route from Mexico,
over the mountains, near the
Mexican border, chained together
like convicts and herded in like
cattle. Sometimes they die
there have been cases known
where those in charge of the
coolies deliberately killed them
and secreted their bodies in order
to avoid capture.
It's a risky business smuggling
Chinamen, risky for the guards,
more risky for the Chinamen who
may be hurled overboard if he's
being smuggled in by boat, or
shot if pursuit is close.
But with success comes huge
profits.
Chinese coolies are willing to
pay as high as $500 each to get
into this country. The money is
advanced by wealthy Chinese in
the United States who want
cheap labor. Once here the
coolies must work out the money
paid for his passage and for
smuggling him in. He is an ab
solute slave. If he balks a quiet
tip slipped to immigration offi
cers and the contrabrand coolie is
put aboard a China liner and
shipped back to "his home.
Ensenada, Mexico, is the clear
ing house of Chinese smuggling.
Mexico has no objections to them
landing there, so they come in
hordes. At Ensenada they are
put in charge of the smugglers,
who run them into the United
States, either by land or by sea.
Recently, under cover of a heavy
fog, a fishing boat sneaked into
San Francisco bay, past war ves
sels, revenue cutters, forts and
police boats, landed" a band of
Chinamen and sneaked out again.
A revenue cutter gave chase,
but the smuggling boat simply,
put on more power and ran away;

than American in their wage

On Jan. 17 Perkins will go to
Washington to appear before the
congressional committee on
Schedule F, the tobacco tariff . He
will urge a high tariff on cigars
made by coolie labor in China and
the Phillipines.
"A higher tariff will be urged
for the protection of those cigar
makers in this country who have
an American standard of living,"
he said. "It must be admitted, of
course, that the vast majority of
the American Tobacco Company
employes get about the same pay
as many of .the Asiatic coolies.
Both get about enough for the
bare necessities of life and are
about two weeks from the poor
house all the time."
CHINA'S PRESIDENT A
LITTLE CORRECTOR
Pekin, Jan. 9. President Yuan
Shi Kai has become the original
little corrector of clothes, morals
and etiquette.
Yuan today issued a proclama:
tion commanding all good Chi
nese to obey the following regu
lations :
To salute, take off the hat and
bow.
At important ceremonies, such
as funerals, marriages and na
tional festivities, take off the hat
and bow three times.
When meeting in the street,
take off the hat without bowing.
Women must obey articles 1
and 2, but they will not taffe off
their hats.
Recently Yuan started a small
riot by commanding all Chinese
women to quit pants and take to
the Western "skoit." .
So far, the good man has not
said what kind of a breakfast
food the Chinese are to eat, nor
yet has he prescribed just how a fj
baby ought to be born.
NEW CHAMP; A DIVORCE
EVERY 6:24 MINUTES
New York, Jan. 9. In a thirty-two
minute struggle, Justice
Newburger of the Superme Court
today annexed the quick-divorce
championship belt from Justice
Giegerich.
Giegerich tried seven divorce
cases in forty-seven minutes and
went home and patted himself on
the back, convinced that no one
could beat his time.
But today Newburger tried five
cases in thirty-two minutes,
which, if you fi,gure it out, is one
divorce in every 6 minutes, 24
seconds, whereas Geigerich only
managed one divorce every 6 min-
utes, 42 seconds.
Untimely appearances in pa
jamas figured in three of the cases
held tried by the new champ, and
the other two were based mainly
on the indiscreet to say the least
of it use of kimonas.
In Lisbon straw hats have be
come very fashionable. Portu
guese find that they serve excel
lently the purpose of a mackin-:
tosh. They are made entirely of
straw, and the wet runs down the
individual straws and so drops to
the ground.

Bhristiansief, BY, Brois,
Saturday, Bth November, 1884.
The Amr. Brig. T. Towner, 187 tons,
Capt. Perkins, arrived here yesterday
morning from New Haven with general
cargo and a deck load of machinery.
The Coolie incident in Trinidad is a very
painful one, of which we shall probably
hear more later. It may have surprised
some of our readers to learn from the tele
gram that there were so many as 8000 Coo
lies in the religious procession which had so
sad an end on the 30th ult, The immi
gration of Coolies into Trinidad is, how
ever, and has been for many years, very
cons‘demble, not less than between two
and three thousand a year. A large pro
portion of these do not return to [udia, but
ol the expiration of their term of inden
gure either renew their contracts for a com-
8% CR Ol AVWYIES
pensation or accept grants of Gorernment
land or set up in the towns and villages as
shopkeepers. Some of them have acquir
ed, as traders, considerable wealth. In
1831 the Census shewed that there were
48,820 Coolies in Trinidad, 2 number equal
to wore than twice the entire population
of Santa Cruz, Os these about half were
employed as labourers on the estates, the
others were either ecarrying on agricufture
en their own account or settled in the
towns and villages.
The procession of which the telegram
speaks was formed wear the town of San
F¥ernando, in which neighbourhood there
seems to be a very large number of these
people, and the San Fernando azetle,
which we receive among our exchanges,
has for some time past been looking anxi
ously forwarded to the Coolie festival. It
appears that the Government of Trinidad
has prohibited processions of every kind,
and unbappily Las, either intentionally or
by oversight, made no provision for the
sunual religious procession held so dear
by the Coolies. The Coolie leaders were
therefore warned that their procession
could not be allowed; but they firmly
refused to give it up, and persisted,
as we now know, to the bitter end. It
seems that the procession was merely a
leligious one, there was no fear of any
outbreak on the part of the Coolies,
they were simply marching to the sey
in order, we are told, to throw in
“ their Tawjahs”, some symbuolical act
doubtless full of meaning to them, and
that done, they would have gone quietly
to their homes.
It is sad to think that an offence such
as theirs should have Leen visited with
80 heavy a punishment.
The Police are, of course, not to be
blamed. They bhad ther orders, and
the procession, once having been forbid
den, must be stopped at all hazards, or the
law would have been brought into con.
tempt ; it will be for the higher authorities
to shew the necessity for making a prohi
bition which has had such n elancholy re
sults.
Another aspect of the question concerns
chiefly the proprietors in ‘Trinidad and
elsewhere who profit by Coolie labour,
and that is the effect which the news will
have in India in deterring intending emi
grants from leaving for the West Indian
colonies,
We take from Lightbourn’s Mail Notes a
brief account of a revolution which has
just taken place in Panama. It may be that
a great improvement for the little State
has been thereby effected, we cannot say ;
and that is not the light in which we are
thinking of it, In a few yeais the Panama
canal will be the channel of an enormous
traffic, and the interests involved will be
long to the great Powers of Europe and
America. Will they be content to allow
the existence of a form of Government so
unstable as that which prevails in the
South and Central American republics, or
will some of them not think it necessary
to establish a protectorate over the isth
mus, as France is alleged by Captain Bed
ford Pim to be intending 1
We must not, however, attach too much
importance to Captain Pim’s allegation, nor
to his other disparaging remarks. Captain
Pim has for manv years been an ardent
advocate of the Nicaragua route, and the
present writer well remembers being pre
sent at a meeting of the Royal Geographi
cal Society in London at which the Cap
tain gave an elaborate account of that
route, illustrated with large maps, and
then stated that he held considerable
concessions in counnection with the con
templated canal.
If the gallant Captain still holds those
concessions it is easy to understand that
he finds out all the weak points of the
Panama scheme, and makes the most of
them. Hence, perhaps, his recent letter to
the “N. Y. Sun” as described in a telegram
which will be found in our last issue.
At a meeting of the Royal Agricultural
and Commercial Scciety of British Guiana
held on Thursday the 9th of October the
President brought forward a motion ‘ask
ing the sanction of the Society to the
opening up of communication with like
societies in Barbados, Trinidad and the
Leeward Islands in order to bring abous a
conference at Barbados to cousider the
whole question of the sugar industry.”
In the course of his speech the President
said :
“The schedule of the Society’s bye-laws
lays down what shall be the objects of the
Society, namely, to endeavour to promote,
as far as possible, the improvement of the
agriculture of the colony, and of every
branch of industry, manufacture, or trade
whereby the resources of the colony are
likely to be developed and increased. [
cannot see that any one present can say
tuat they consider the trade of British
Guiana or the West Indian islands is in
sach a flourishing condition that they
could afford to quietly fold their hands
and say: “We will do nothing.” There is
not the slightest doubt in the minds of
everyone that the West Indies are passing
through a severe crisis—and we are just
at the commencement, and have not gone
very far down the road. I believe we shall
experience greater difficulties than we are
now experiencing before brighter times
come. And in order to prevent what
would be a great calamity to our neigh
bours and a serious matter for ourselves,
we should endeavour to do anything and
everything to prevent that crisis being so
severe, by a meeting of delegates—persons
who fnlly understand how to deal with the
agricultural and commercial interests of
the West India colonies. In asking the
Society to lend its aid to a matter of this
sort, L might say that the conference should
be composed of persons who are deeply
concerned in the sugar industry of the
colonies, so that they could thoroughly
discuss and ventilate the subject; such
subjects as those connected with the cen
tral factories and usines, which, if properly
carried on would still attract British capi
tal to the West Indies. I think that the
existir g commercial relations not only with
England, but also with America and
foreign nations require alteration, and if
altered, a vetter and more prosperous
state of things than at the pregent moment
exists would be brought about. If dele
gates are sent from here to Barbados,
they would be, as far as this Society is
councerned, restricted entirely to this ques
tion of commerce and agriculture. [ think
it is one of the duties of this Society to
promote the agricultural and commereial
welfare of those colonies, and as president
ot this Society, I do not think that I have
trespassed or infringed upon the charter
granted to us, in biinging forward such a
motion as the present one.”
Notwithstanding these excellent argu.-
ments for a conference the motion was lost
because the charter granted the Society
does not permit of its entering on political
discussions, and most of the members
thought that such a conference wonld
prove to be in a great measure a political
meeting, Possibly the suggestion may be
taken up by one ot the other leading co
lonies.
A curious smuggling story will be found
among our Demerara items. To under
stand it clearly we must remember that
Georgetown and New Amsterdam are both
ports of British Guiana, the capitals of the
countries of Demerara and Berbice res
pectively. Surinam is the neighbouring
Duteh ¢olony. A Portuguese firm pre
tends to ship 800 cases of duty-paid gin to
New Amsterdam, and gets eertificates &ec.
from the Georgetown t'nstoms to that
effect. In reality, however, the schooner
goes out empty, and meets at sea another
schooner with 800 cases of gin from Bauri
nam, which of course has paid no duty.
These she takes on board, and lands at
New Amsterdam, under cover of the
Georgetown papers, as duty paid, thus
escaping a duty of 82.50 per gallon, say in
all about $6,000. The Surinam schoonerin
the meanwhile manages to lose her pa pers,
which doubtlese would have revealed the
awkward fact that she onght to have had
800 eases of gin on board, and then runs
into Georgetown. ‘l'he Customs authori
ties in the meanwhile get some informa
tion, and Dboth vessels are seized. The
fine against the offending firm we are told
has been laid at $68,000. From a later
number of the Argosy it appears that this
large sum includes fines for other offences
which have later come to light, and the
writer in the Argosy thinks that part of it
will have to be abandoned from want of
sufficient evidence.
It may be thought a hopeful sign that
Mr. Lubbock (the telegramn says Bir John)
and Mr. Ohlsen have, according to tele
grams received from New York, gone to
Washingron to negotiste a commercial
treaty between the United States and the
British West Indies. The probability is,
however, that it is only an indication of
the desire of the British Celonial Office to
shew the colonists that the magnates who
rule there are not forgetful of the interests

REPORT OF THE
U. S. GOVERNMENT
The Resources and Ingenuity of
the Planters Taxed to Secure
Proper Laborers.
The question of labor In Hawaii is
dealt with at some length in a report on
the agricultural resources and capabili
ties of that Territory, recently issued by
the office of experiment stations of the
Department of Agriculture. For half a
century, it is said, the resources and In
genuity of the planters of the islands
have been taxed In devising the best
means of procuring laborers suitable for
their work. Special commissioners have
been dispatched to distant parts of the
globe for the purpose of obtaining the
desired Immigration. Earnest efforts
have been made in the way ot carefully
prepared reports and extens ve corre
spondence. Large sums of money have
been expended for costly voyages in the
hope of obtaining permanent additions to
the population of the islands which
would develop and maintain the growing
agriculture.
The report continues:
"From the first arrival of coolies, ta
1852, up to the present time there has
been no cessation in the arduous efforts
to obtain an adequate supply of labor
for the plantations. The Royal Hawaii
an Agricultural Society, established in
1850, issued a circular stating that the
introduction of. coolie labor from China
to supply the places of the rapidly de
creasing native population was a subject
o"f great importance. In 1852 the first In
troduction of coolies was made and the
experiment was satisfactory. They prov
ed able and willing laborers and quieted
for a while all apprehension of future
trouble in obtaining labor. Other car
goes were soon sent for and received.
"But while the coolies were and are
good workers It was soon discovered that
laborers imported for plantations could
not be relied upon as permanent settlers
and homeseekers, and were, therefore,
from a State standpoint, very undesir
able immigrants. The planters wanted
laborers for profit; the King desired per
manent settlers for the benefit of the
country. To bring in Immigrants re- i
. . I 5 f. . -J .UlnU 1 ' ' 1
could supply, but they were unwilling to
burden themselves with the trouble and
expense of families. Hence the plans of
the King failed. In 1859 a few South
Sea Islanders were landed on Kauai to
work on a plantation under contract.
They resembled Hawaiians, were educat
ed, and had Christian names. It was
hoped that this beginning would be the
means, ultimately, of repopulating the
islands and supplying the needed labor,
but the hope was never realized. In 1863
another cry for importation -of labor was
heard, but the inquiry was everywhere
made: Whom and how? The importa
tion of white men as laborers was inad
missible; ditto with negroes. The coolie
was an undesirable citizen and as a la
borer of no great value. Private plant
ers, would import only men, rejecting
women and children. Laborers could not
be obtained from Pacific islands ; there
fore resort must again be had to China.
BUREAU OF IMMIGRATION.
"These abuses gave birth to the coolie
laws of the United States, and simulta
neously in Hawaii a bureau of immigra
tion was established, taking the business
of importing laborers from the hands of
the planters and placing it under the
charge of the kingdom. The Hawaiians
claim that no such iniquity as above
mentioned ever disgraced the efforts of
their planters in their work of import
ing laborers. The "coolie system as
known elsewhere never existed on the
islands;- the law between employer and
employe known as the 'master and ser
vant law' was mild, equitable and com
pulsory for the specific fulfillment of
contracts.
"Efforts were made to secure immi
grants from India, Japan and Malaysia,
and the Dutch and English East Indies.
All proved abortive, after much discus
sion, diplomacy, and a large expenditure
of time and money, except with Japan.
"A shipload of Japanese reached the
islands n 1369.
CO-OPERATION TRIED.
"A new and untried system of co-operative
labor was introduced on a small
scale in 1870. Thirty white men from the
States were carried over to labor on the
plantations to receive as compensation
one-half of the crop made. On account
of the drought, inexperience, etc., the
co-operative experiment was far from
being a decided success.
"Again attention was directed to the
Azores and the possibility of obtaining
desirable immigrants therefrom. Italy
was also considered as a source of sup
ply of future laborers. But nothing tan
gible was accomplished. In July, 1876,
the treaty of reciprocity between the
Hawaiian Kingdom and the United
States was ratified. It created great joy
and much inspiration in every planter In
the islands. Extensive improvements
were undertaken at once and energetic
efforts made to secure more laborers.
"In 1878 the first batch of Portuguese
from Madeira, secured after patient ef
forts, was landed in Honolulu, followed
soon after by a cargo of South Sea Isl
anders, all of whom proved satisfactory
to the planters. In 1881 two vessels filled
with Norwegians arrived. But few of
them were agriculturists, and therefore
were soon dissatisfied with life on plan
tations. "The propriety of introducing negroes
from the Southern States was discussed
by the board, with the result of a de
cided opposition to the scheme. More
cargoes of South Sea Islanders were In
troduced, followed soon after by several
vessel loads of Germans.
"Since 1886 up to the time of annexa
tion the introduction of Chinese, Japan
ese and Portuguese has been made to
, meet the wants of the islands. The Jap
anese and Portuguese have been brought
In mainly on contract, and having com
pleted the time of service required by
the latter they have either renewed ser
vice or been returned to their homes.
The Chinese have come on their own
accord."

text:
he should give us employment. There is yet work upon
the bridge, and we be painters."
Lai Buksh took up the scheme smoothly. '"Yet, see
ing that we have met with thee. an<l that MacManus
Sahib is thy friend, we will fill in the hole now. Thou
wilt say to him, 'Friend, here be two who failed where
I made success my own?give them work for thy
friend's sake.' Surely, he will hasten to give thee
pleasure."
TTHEX Chunder Sen remembered everything. His
anger and despair rode him with spurs on the way
to madness. In the name of all his gods, he wished that
he had never stumbled upon this conspiracy' His own
little world?compact" of the trust of the helpless, his
own pity and generosity, and their mutual affection ?
was crumbling into ruin. What did he care if the Gov
ernor and every European in Amrakutch were blown
sky high?so long as only MacManus went with them?
How much of all this he had said, or left unsaid, he
did not know when he came to himself again. But he
did know that'4ie had voiced his suspicions of these two
men overplainly, and that there could be no more
feigning ignorance as to the meaning of their presence
on the line. His rage burned itself out suddenly, and in
the cold ashes of it was a dreadful fear. He sprang up;
but Ram Chundra's compelling touch was on his shoul
der, and he sank under it abjectly.
"Now the matter is easy," said Ram Chundra com
fortably. "It is true that we came to do this thing, as
thou sayest; but it shall be a greater thing than we had
conceived. Let the mine, then, be laid not here, but be
neath the station, where all the Sahibs shall be gathered
together. We be thy coolies, (J Chunder Sen. See that
we have time, and that none overlcx>k our labor."
Clearly there was no choice. There was a flood of
horror, of bloodshed, to plunge into; but on the other
side was life, free of MacManus and the forever infa
mous bet. The alternative he read in the eves of the
so-called coolies. These men had come with their lives
in their hands. He put his cold fingers to his bruised
throat shudderinglv. But for the merest chance, he
would have been a swollen-faced corpse lying beside the
line by now?or across it, so that the first train might
account for his violent end. Yes, the
fierce question in those intent eyes
had to be answered. His darkened,
puffy lips formed his first words to
them again:
"Good?very good!"
Xo more was needed. Ram Chun
dra took command.
"Go thou before us down the line,
and we will follow, under thy pro
tection. We have dynamite in the
bushes here, and we will bring it to
a safe place near the station. Come,
Brother!"
?"THERE were several stunted
bushes within a few yards, and
as he approached them a man rose
from their shelter and sped silently
and with such swiftness that the
darkness swallowed him up almost
at once, and made pursuit impos
sible. They just saw that he was
naked, save for a loincloth; and Lai
Buksh thought he had seen a long,
white beard: but he could not be
quite sure. At first Chunder Sen
shook like a leaf, and his teeth
chattered with insane fright; but
the other men laughed at him until
he braced himself up to a show of
calmness. It was but a coolie, they
said, an old coolie who could not
have understood the English they
had been speaking, even had he
1 >een close enough to overhear. Also,
i: he was a spy, he would give notice
that at this part of the line there
might be danger, and the police
would look, and find a hole, and
no more. Let them look!
Chunder Sen nodded, and hurried
away back toward the station. His
comfort was that in the halflight the
hidden man could not have recog
nized him or have seen his compan
ions clearly. He found the night
watchman at the station, and told
him that the two new coolies wait
ing beyond had been taken on to
work there. Then he left them, and
walked leisurely in the direction of
his own quarters.
He had to pass the chief's rough
bungalow. It was lighted up, and
through the open doorway Mac
Manus could be seen at dinner, his
servant standing behind his chair.
As Chunder Sen looked on them from
Lying on the earthen floor was the frail
husk of him. cast off by much suffering.
the svmbolic blac1 n without, a voice ( lose bcsi<le
him set every nerve in his overwrought body jar ring
tumultuouslv. Then the panic subsided into a deadly
quiet as an old man came out into the light shed by
MacM anus's lamps, and salaamed?for it was a lean
old man, garbed as a coolie, and having a long, white
beard. Without doubt it was the man who had been
hidden in the bushes. But he did not seem to recognize
Chunder Sen.
"I would speak with MacManus Sahib," he said
breathlessly; '"but they who serve him drive me away.
If thou art his servant?"
A sting of anger forced Chunder Sen into speech.
"I am the Chief Specification Sahib," lie said curtly.
The old man was speaking in English, with a purer
accent than that of Chunder Sen himself. He was no
coolie. "Then lead me unto him. He will give thanks
to thee also when he hath heard that which I have come
far to tell. Look thou ?when the Sahib's father was
padre in Kanara I was clerk in the telegraphs?even I!
I have borne MacManus Baba in these arms?and
now he is a great man. Say unto him, I beseech thee,
that Govind Vinayak waits, and would speak with him
?for his life's sake!"
Chunder Sen could scarcely control the tremblings
that seized upon him again now more strongly than
ever. He was cold all over in spite of the steamy
heat. The whisper came hissingly from between his
unsteady lips. "What is it, what is it? Tell me. I?
I am the friend of MacManus Sahib. Whence comest
thou?"
The old man had not recognized Chunder Sen?that
was only too true. He gave up his story simply as a
child. "From Amedhar. And to us there came one
many months ago, speaking of killing the Sahibs so
that such as he should be Sahibs in their place. All
this we passed with negligence until he left "us, saying
that Boynton Sahib should be killed?and he was
killed. Also that Grant Sahib be killed?and he was
killed. Then he returned to us, boasting greatly, and
said that the Burra Sahib would come to this place
upon a day that is tomorrow, and that he would t>e
killed. Thereupon I made report to the police in
spector?who returned reply that it was not his dis
tri. t. it being seven l?>n, lays' marches from here. 1
laid information with the District Judge Sahib?and lie
reproached me, saying that it was such as I who. talking
of treason, made treason. Then I learned that the one
whom the Burra Sahib journeyed hither to do honor
unto was my MacManus Sahib?my chota Sahib, that
is a great man since. Therefore I have come afoot as
a poor man to warn him, lest his day of joy be turned
into bitterness by these doers of evil."
There was no response at all. Chunder Sen stood and
stared, his mind a sheer blank of terror. The old man
stooped and whispered:
"Behold, I came by the new line, and there I saw
two men making a pit. I hid in the bushes, and
thereunder were sticks of dynamite. And again came
another to the two, and it was said between them that
the mine should belaid not between the rails, but under
the station. And one of the men was he who came to
my village saying that the Burra Sahib should be killed
here, on the day that is tomorrow. Now take me unto
mv Sahib!"
M acMAXUS had just finished dinner when Chun
der Sen came in. rolling his eyes, and working his
hands nervously in the air. He jumped up and pushed
his chair back, with a brusk demand for information.
"What's wrong? What the?"
"Oh, Mr. MacManus," spluttered Chunder Sen, saw
ing at the air, "there is the very old Dickens wrong!
You will be hardly creditable when you hear. Xo more
less than design to wreck his Excellency's train tomor
row! Individually I ascertained the culprit, an old
man, but one of strength. A mile up line he was digging
away sleepers, and I, irrespective of personal welfare,
maintained the capture."
"Where is he?" asked MacManus sharply.
"Under certain pretenses I decoyed him until several
coolies were obtainable, and then, in spite of mighty
struggling, we incarcerated him in one of the huts?
the key being herewith." Chunder Sen showed it lying
in the palm of his hand. "And if I might venture ad
vice, Mr. MacManus, I would say avoid unrestful
rumorings by keeping him locked up until after advent
of Governor tomorrow. Then, if I am wrong, 1 accept
all blameworthiness."
It was not the first time that
MacManus had taken the law into
his own hands, and in an instant he
decided that Chunder Sen was right.
The whole thing might turn out to
be a foolish scare; but if he in
formed the authorities it would cer
tainly cause a worthy but highly
nervous .gentleman to deviate from
plans that were most important to
all Amrakutch that he should carry
out. And even if mischief had l>een
intended they had secured one man,
and now had warning that would
keep them on the alert. So he sat
down again and selected a cigar; for
he had had a long day of it, and was
tired.
"Very well," he said, "I'll see into
it later. Tell the police to be on the
lookout, and have the line patroled
all night. Examine all the ap
proaches yourself at daybreak. We'll
leave nothing to chance?though
personally 1 should say it is some
old fool playing at plots in order to
bring his private grievances to light.
Time enough to send him to be ex
amined by the magistrate after the
Governor has gone."
Knowing the thoroughness with
which Chunder Sen invariably car
ried out all work intrusted to him,
MacManus made no personal sur
vey of the line next morning, nor of
the station. It was enough that he
found the line being patroled ac
cording to orders, when he came
upon the scene, and that Chunder
Sen was superintending everything
even more busily and fussily than
usual. So, MacManus, who was
going to entertain his Excellency
and sundry guests at tiffin in the big
marquee attached to the tempor
ary station, devoted himself to prep
arations for an exceptionally gor
geous hospitality.
DAM CHUXDRA and his com
rade sat peacefully in a disused
toolhut about one hundred yards up
the line, guarding a storage battery.
They had laid insulated wires
underground very skilfully and ex
peditiously during the night, and
were in a position to explode with

Wednesday, 12th November, 1884
An extraordinary meeting of the
Colonial Council was called for
Monday, chicfly for the purpose of
electing a chairman in the place of
His Excellency Vice Governor
Andersen, who by virtue of his
position has been compelled to resign
his seat in the Council. The
Government Secretary, Councillor
Van Brakle, was present at the
meeting, and Councillor Faber as
Vice Chairman occupied the chair.
The letter of resignation was read,
and the resignation subsequently
accepted, so that there will shortly
be a new election of a member for
Frederiksted.
I'he Vice Chairman read the let
ter of the Council’s Chairman cob
veying the good wishes of the Coun
cil to His Excellency the Governor
on the occasion of his leaving for
Europe, and also His Excellency’s
reply.
The Council then proceeded to
elect a new chairman, Councillor
Faber being elected with seven out
of nine votes.
Councillor laber thanked the
Members for the honour done him
and promised to performtheduties of
the post to the best of his abilities
for the short time remaining of the
present session. Heo also said that
he felt sure he could count on the
forbearance of members in regard
to any deficiencies in his efforts.
The Council then elected a
member of the School Board in
place of the Vice Governor, resigned,
Judge Hiinschell being elected.
4 i . . ~
The Commiiteo to assist the Tax
Commission was then elected,
namely for Christiansted, Oapt.
Boye and Mr. Jas. P. Bruley; for
Frederiksted, Mr. W. I. Brown
and Mr. Masterson.
Finally the Council proceeded to
the election of a Vice Chairman 1
place of Councillor Faber, and
Judge Hinschell was elected.
Thereafter the mecting was closed.
At ameeting of the Board of Trustees
of the New St. Croix Savings Bank on
Monday, Mr, Dendtler was elected as a
member of the Board, and Mr. Royal
Bookkeeper Leschly was elected President
of the Bank in the place of the Vice
Governor, retired.
To the Editor of the “St. Croix Avis.”
Sir,
With reference to the remarks in your
issue of the Bth inst, on the recentjdisturb
ances in I'rinidad, the ¢ Tadjahs” (more
properly ¢ Taziyas”), which the untortu
nate Coolies were prevented from throwing
into the sea, are models, generally made
of paper and tinsel, of the tomb of Hasan
and Husain, the martyred sons of Fatima
the daughter of the Prophet Muhammed.
The first month of the Muhammadan
year is called Muharram, i. e., sacred, and
the feast of the Mubarram, concluding
with the throwing of the 'Taziyas into the
river, is one which excites the utmost
religious zeal and fanaticism on the part
of the followers of the Prophet.
The feast of the Mubarram, as carried
on in Trinidad, may have developed into
license and excess which the Authorities
refused to countenance.
The word ‘ coolie;” now so familiar in
the West Indies, in reality signifies a
slave, and in the East Indies is applied
to porters and laborers generally.
I am ete.
H.

New York, Nov. sth.—Washington news
announces that the result of the Presiden
tial election is not yet decided, but it is
thought that Cleveland, the Democratic
candidate, will probably be successful.
The returns are still incomplete.
Telegraphic intelligence from the Sou
dan states that Lord Wolseley believes
Khartoum is still safe.
France, in reply to the American sugges
tions for mediation in the Chinese com
plication, declares that the minimum terms
acceptable would be the unreserved accept
ance by China of the Tientsin treaty and
the payment of the indemnity.
Further Cholera advices report twelve
cases at Nantes and seven at Yport yester
day.
New York, 6th Nov.—The excitement in
America in connection with the Presiden
tial election is unparalleled, owing to the
doubt as to the probable successful can
didate. The crisis is exceedingly grave.
The earlier despatches claimed that New
York State had voted for the Democratic
ticket, practically deciding the election in
favor of Cleveland. Later reports; however,
affirm that Blaine’s plurality of votes
throughout the State obliterates Cleveland’s
in the South Harlem River including the
cities of New York and Brooklyn. Sumilar
conflicting rumours which are circulated
respecting the resalt in other doubtful
States increase the anxiety to fever heat.
All the estimates are so conflicting that
the result will perhaps remain doubttul tor
several days. Both sides bring the charge
of wholesale fraud, but even partisan
papers counsel the country to wait in
peaceful expectation ot a settiement.
The passengers by the Rotterdam stea
mer “Maasdam” which was veceatly burnt
at sea have reached New York.
M. IFerry has informed Earl Granville
that Ifiance will make peaece with Cnina,
if the latter shall cause no fiether eXpense
and will pay forty millions of dollars as an
indemnity.
There were thirteen cases of cholera and
six deaths at Nantes yesterday.
London, Nov. 6th.—The stock of sugar
at the four home ports on the Ist inst. was
243,000 tons, as against 20L000 tons for
corresponding period last year.
New York, Nov. 7th.—The excitement in
America continues indeseribable, All
depended on how New York State would
cast its thirty six votes in the Ilectoral
College.
The delayed returns indicated that
Blaine electors had been chosen by a small
plurality of less than two thousand ina
total vote of over a million, but it is now
probable that Cleveland will be victorious
with a plurality ot some hundreds.
The “Pall Mall Gazotte,” in an editorial,
remarks upon the surprising testimony of
the value of Democratic institutions as
manifested by the fact that there has been
no alteration of confidence on the European
Exchanges.
The Mayor of New York is urging the
expediency of holding a conference of
Mayors of the ports on the American sea
board for the consideration of Panama
affairs,
The death is announced of Mr. Fawcett,
the British Postmaster General. The
House of Conmons adjourned after a brief
sitting, during which tributes by the
Marquis of Hartington, Sir Stafford
Northeote and Mr, Justice McCarthy were
delivered in honor of the deceased Minister.
The survivors of the yacht *Mignonette,”
who were charged with caunibalism, have
been found guilty, but were allowed Dbail
pending the consideration of the law by
the Superior Court.
In the French Chamber of Deputies, M.
Ferry announced that England has offered
to mediate for the settlement of the China
troubles,
The French Senate yesterday passed the
clause of the bill abolishing life Senatorship.
New York, Nov. SBth—According to the
the latest news of the Presidential election
it is generally conceded that Cleveland
has carried New York, but the Republi
cans insist on an oflicial court.
The Franchise Bill was yesterday read a
second time in the IHonse of Commons by a
majority of one hundred and forty.
Cholera intelligence announces that the
epidemic has reappeared at Naples. Seven
deaths have occurred at Toulon, three at
Nantes and three at Oran yesterday.
Twenty one genuine cases of Asiatio
cholera are reported to have appeared in
Paris with thirteen deaths. The disease
is confined to the Rue Marguerite St. An
toine in the quarter chiefly occuplqd by
the rag pickers. The city, however, is not
alarmed
The French Senate has passed the sec
tions of the revision Bill providing for the
election of two hundred and twenty five
Senators from the Departments, the Col o
nies sending seventy five members to the
Senate, the term for sitting being reduced
to nine years instead of for life.
Information has been received in . New
York ot the failure of a large sugar and
cotton merchant named Dammler of . Ba
afiat. :

The Coolie Festival in Trinidad.
The following passage appears in
the San Fernando Gazette under date
of the 25th ult, just five days belore
the catastrophe described by tele
gram.
“Uhe moon is now moving towards
its first quarter. With its first ap
pearance came the distinet and well
known sound of the Coolie gong,
which reminds us that “ Coming
eveuts cast their shadows before.”
"Thesesounds, towhichthe Creole ear
has become so well acenstomed, ure
theindieators of the near approach of
the Coolie festival of the Hosein.
tleard at this particular time they
give an open denial to the assurances
advanced by some that there wiil be
no Hosé. It has been rumoured
that the anxiety on this account has
brought the Kxecutive Council into
deliberation on the probable issue of
events; let us hope that there will be
neither cause of fear, reproach, or
regret,”
The Festival of Hosein is one of the
most solemn and touching religious festi-*
vals known to the world. We are indebt
ed to a correspondent for a brief account,
which will be read with interest, of the
“Tadjahs” mentioned in the telegrams.

Mr. Whiteley, ornithologist, whose
last expedition to Roraima added a
large number of new birds to the
species known to collectors, has re
turned from England to the colony,
on his way to the far inleri_ox,.,yvherc '
he will be engaged for several months
in the same field of labour. He has '
brought with him a fine collection of
bird skins as an offering to the Muse
um, where they can be inspected ;
and we believe he is prepared during
the journey he is now about to begin
to make a collection on behalf of this
colony, it the Government will, as a
matter of business, contribute propor- |
tionally towards the expenses of the
expedition. Os course, he is going,
whether our Government will or will
not contribute ; but he has, we under- -
stand, given them the opportunity of
accepting his offer. As our Museum
of local fauna oughtto be made as
complete as circumstances will allow, ¢
the Government will do wisely to ac
cept Mr. Whiteley's offer.
